None of the options directly address participant feedback thresholds — if even the scheduled three-night full PSG protocol causes perceptible sleep fragmentation that participants report, you may be capturing artifactually disrupted events rather than authentic parasomnia patterns.

levers.name: Dual-Rater Scoring Allocation and Bottleneck Relief
levers.lever_classification: execution — scoring workflow architecture and rater deployment
levers.consequences: Requiring two independent AASM-trained raters to score every captured event ensures inter-rater reliability but creates a severe annotation bottleneck; if 70–80% of admits yield ≥1 event and event morphology is complex, the 3-person technician team plus a clinical psychologist cannot sustain real-time and post-hoc dual scoring without external contract labor or AI-assisted pre-screening. The choice determines publication credibility, operational throughput, and contingency cost.
options: Restrict dual-scoring to a core subset (e.g., all first admitted events per participant, plus a random 30% of subsequent events) and score remaining events single-blind by the senior technician, reporting the dual-scored subset's agreement and acknowledging single-scored variance in the methods.

Contract three part-time independent raters (e.g., sleep technologists from regional clinics) paid per event scored, rotating blind assignment so no rater is consistently paired, and reserve in-house scoring for quality-control spot-checks and inter-rater training calibration every 4 weeks.

Develop a two-tier scoring workflow: junior technicians flag and annotate probable events with a confidence score in real time, then the clinical psychologist and one senior rater jointly review only high-confidence flags and resolve disagreements on complex cases, treating technician pre-screening as a time-saving filter rather than full dual independence.
levers.review: The core tension is speed versus methodological purity: dual-scoring is gold-standard but expensive and slow, while single-scoring or tiered confidence-filtering introduces rater bias and publication-credibility risk. The options assume sufficient available independent raters in the Bonn region; if clinical sleep technologists are scarce or reluctant to score outside their own facility, contract labor costs and timeline delays will exceed budget and delay publications.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Timing and Capacity Risk Appetite
levers.lever_classification: operations — residential capacity expansion trigger and growth trajectory
levers.consequences: Maintaining 8 active suites for the full 36 months preserves operational focus and allows the core team to master low-volume protocols, but caps total enrollment at approximately 50–70 participants and limits event yield, potentially underpowering Aim 2 phenotyping analyses. Expanding to 10–12 suites in year 2 (with additional night technician hires) multiplies event capture and publication potential but demands renovation capital mid-project, introduces staffing onboarding friction, and risks operational chaos if data-quality or safety metrics are unstable.
options: Remain at 8 active suites through month 36, treat expansion as a phase-two activity contingent on successful grant renewal, and accept the tighter enrollment ceiling as a controlled, publication-friendly cohort design.

Expand to 10 active suites in month 18 upon evidence of stable safety and data-quality metrics (e.g., <5% false alarm rate, <2% adverse events, >90% annotation completion within 48 hours), recruiting and onboarding a fourth night technician concurrently to distribute workload.

Commit to 12 active suites by month 12, execute the renovation and technician hiring in parallel with pilot operations (months 6–12), and accept short-term operational friction and higher early-stage error rates as the cost of maximizing longitudinal event density for publication competitiveness.
levers.review: The tension is between operational robustness and scientific ambition: early expansion (option 3) compounds staffing complexity, training overhead, and technical debt during the methodological validation phase, yet delays mean lost event-capture months and a weaker grant-renewal package. The options do not account for the housing market in Bonn or ongoing renovation financing logistics — if the residential property has limited expansion potential (e.g., only 8 suites can be safely retrofitted within existing building code), some options become infeasible mid-project.

levers.name: Enrollment Inclusion Criteria Stringency and Productivity-Admission Tolerance
levers.lever_classification: execution — participant inclusion stringency and admission-outcome risk tolerance
levers.consequences: Strict inclusion criteria (e.g., ≥4 self-reported episodes per month, confirmed bed-partner collateral, prior PSG documentation) minimize unproductive admissions (those yielding no captured events) but reduce eligible referral population by an estimated 30–40%, slowing recruitment velocity and enrollment targets. Relaxed criteria (e.g., ≥1 episode per month reported, collateral optional, interview-only confirmation) accelerate enrollment but increase unproductive-admission rate above the 20% protocol ceiling, triggering costly recruitment-criteria review and protocol extensions.
options: Maintain the current ≥2 episodes per month threshold with mandatory bed-partner confirmation or prior PSG, accepting unproductive admissions up to the 20% ceiling as a buffer, and activate the recruitment-review trigger only if the rate persistently exceeds 25% across a 10-participant rolling window.

Lower the inclusion threshold to ≥1 episode per month with collateral history optional (interview-confirmed only), monitor unproductive admission rate intensively (weekly rather than rolling aggregates), and implement a mid-point protocol pause at month 18 to reassess event-capture yield before continuing year-two enrollment scaling.

Implement a two-tier enrollment pathway: tier 1 admits high-confidence participants (≥4 episodes/month, collateral, prior PSG) for standard 2–8 week stays; tier 2 enrolls lower-confidence participants (≥1 episode/month, interview only) for shorter exploratory 1-week visits with the explicit goal of collecting baseline nocturnal data even if no events occur, treating tier-2 data as method-validation rather than event-yield cohort.
levers.review: The core tension is recruitment velocity versus event yield efficiency: relaxed criteria flood the unit with participants but generate false negatives (null admissions that consume resources), while strict criteria preserve data quality but risk missing the 36-month enrollment target and triggering external review. The options assume collateral availability is the binding constraint, but in practice, participant self-report bias and recollection errors may be driving unproductive admissions — none of the options directly propose pre-admission structured sleep diaries or actigraphy screening to improve participant self-awareness.

levers.name: Real-Time Safety Decision Authority and Crisis Escalation
levers.lever_classification: governance — real-time safety authority distribution and crisis-response latency
levers.consequences: Granting the night technician authority to halt monitoring, discharge a participant, or escalate to on-call physician during suspected safety events (e.g., confusion, injury risk, fall) enables rapid response but risks inconsistent decision-making across shifts and potential liability exposure if judgment is wrong. Centralizing all real-time decisions through the PI or clinical psychologist (on-call rotation) ensures consistency but delays response time by 15–30 minutes during night hours, potentially worsening outcomes.
options: Empower night technicians as the de facto first responders: develop a three-tier decision tree (routine observation and annotation, direct participant check-in and reassurance, immediate on-call physician consultation) with technician authority to escalate independently, supported by fortnightly safety-decision simulations and a 24-hour incident-review debrief protocol.

Implement a hybrid model where technicians gather real-time data and context during suspected events (vital signs, behavioral video, environmental sensor logs) and relay to an on-call sleep physician via secure video call for joint assessment within 10 minutes; technician may act unilaterally only if imminent injury risk is clear (fall, head trauma, cardiac dysrhythmia).

Require all real-time safety decisions to funnel through a single clinical coordinator who is trained in sleep-disorder recognition and protocols, stationed on-site during peak hours (8 PM–6 AM) and on-call outside those hours, serving as a human buffer between technician observation and physician escalation to ensure consistent adjudication.
levers.review: The tension is responsiveness versus consistency: night technicians are present and have real-time sensory data, but are not physicians; physicians ensure clinical judgment but may be 30 minutes away from effective intervention. The options underspecify the liability framework — if a participant is discharged or restrained based on technician judgment and later claims harm, who bears the legal and institutional risk, and does University Hospital Bonn's insurance cover independent technician decision-making authority?

levers.name: Phase-Two Grant Narrative and Clinical-Translation Ambition
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — grant-renewal scope and translational-research trajectory
levers.consequences: Pitching phase two as 'methods expansion and larger event cohort' (conservative) aligns with 36-month publications and external-review gatekeeping but signals modest scientific scope, limiting appeal to larger federal funders and reducing leverage for pharma partnerships. Proposing 'intervention trials (e.g., cognitive behavioral therapy, pharmacologic pre-screening) and longitudinal prognostic modeling' (ambitious) attracts higher-tier grant competitions and pharma interest but requires committing to designs not yet informed by phase-one data, risking overpromise and timeline slippage.
options: Construct the phase-two proposal as a natural follow-up to phase-one phenotyping: 'longitudinal event trajectories and within-person trigger analysis in an expanded 40–50 participant cohort,' positioning the work as foundational science rather than intervention-ready, targeting Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG) continuation funding and conservative pharma co-sponsorship.

Design phase two as a hybrid: publish phase-one methods and phenotyping work in months 24–36, then launch a parallel small proof-of-concept intervention arm (e.g., structured sleep hygiene coaching or beta-blocker pre-treatment in a 15-participant subgroup) during year two, testing both the intervention feasibility and the biomarker predictors needed for larger trials.

Explicitly scope phase two as 'clinical translation and diagnostic decision-support:' develop the event-triage algorithm into a decision-support tool for neurologists managing parasomnia patients, validate against real-world clinic referrals outside Bonn, and partner with a pharma or diagnostics company to fund a multi-center pilot, positioning the residential unit as the training and benchmarking core rather than the recruitment engine.
levers.review: The tension is scientific ambition versus execution risk: ambitious phase-two goals (interventions, multi-center translation) improve long-term funding and pharma appeal but demand successful phase-one phenotyping and algorithm development on a compressed timeline. The options assume that phase-one event-capture and phenotyping will be sufficiently mature by month 24 to warrant intervention design; if event yield falls short or inter-rater reliability is poor, the phase-two narrative becomes threadbare and delays the grant resubmission cycle.

levers.name: Bed Partner Involvement Depth and Collateral-Data Inclusion
levers.lever_classification: methodology — bed-partner collateral involvement and dyadic observational-data collection
levers.consequences: Recruiting bed partners or household members to reside in the facility or visit regularly for in-person collateral interviews and observational data strengthens event contextual richness (who witnessed what, sleep disruption, environmental triggers) and improves participant engagement, but adds significant recruitment complexity, housing cost, and participant-privacy entanglement. Relying on pre-admission structured telephone interviews with bed partners reduces operational burden but yields thinner, retrospective collateral data vulnerable to recall bias.
options: Restrict bed-partner involvement to pre-admission structured clinical interviews conducted by phone or in the sleep clinic, with optional post-discharge debrief calls; do not invite partners to reside in or visit the facility, avoiding housing and privacy complications and keeping the unit as a participant-only research space.

Offer optional bed-partner overnight visits (one per week, separate sleeping quarters) with structured morning debriefs; compensate partners at €20 per visit, formalize confidentiality agreements, and use their observations as secondary narrative corroboration (not primary event-identification data) to enrich phenotyping context.

Actively recruit bed partners as co-residents during the participant's stay (especially for couples willing to share a larger suite if available), providing them with the same consent and daily activity structure, and treat the partner as a secondary observer and data source; collect bed-partner sleep and movement data as a control-comparison, yielding dyadic behavioral data not otherwise available.
levers.review: The tension is methodological richness versus operational simplicity and participant autonomy: including partners improves contextual understanding of parasomnia triggers and witnessed behaviors, but introduces a third party into intimate nocturnal data collection, complicates privacy safeguards, and may alter participant behavior more than a clinician observer. The options assume bed partners are willing and available; if recruitment targets are not met because eligible participants lack available partners or partners decline to participate, the collateral-data strategies become moot and the facility risks operating as a single-observer model.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Classification**
   - `lever_classification`: a brief phrase with the category and what the lever decides.
   - Format: "category — what this lever decides".
   - Categories: methodology, execution, governance, dissemination, product, operations.
   - Examples: "governance — who oversees the review process", "methodology — which data collection approach to use", "execution — how to sequence the rollout phases".

3. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

4. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

5. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

6. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

7. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration

File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
